text: Perineal sinus is a late complication of radical surgery in the pelvis for both benign disease and cancer. An excision of these sinuses can often result in substantial tissue loss. Previous plastic myofascial flap techniques designed to improve healing in the perineum can often result in substantial late complications such as incisional hernia. Therefore, the use of a fasciocutaneous flap, such as the inferior gluteal artery perforator flap, could allow improved perineal healing with reduced donor site morbidity. This video aims to describe some technical considerations in use of the eye gap flap for reconstruction in the perineum after substantial tissue loss.

This forty-five-year-old man underwent a single-stage colectomy and formation of an ileal pouch at another institution, with substantial post-operative morbidity. He then came to pouch excision some two years ago and was left with a chronic perineal sinus, which on MRI was confirmed to extend to the coccyx. The perineal sinus, in this case, was ten centimeters deep.

The surgeon begins the operation with a teardrop incision extending cranially and caudally in the midline. The perineal sinus is dissected down to its base using a combination of sharp dissection and electrocautery. The mobilized sinus is carefully examined to ensure complete clearance of affected tissue, often necessitating the excision of the base of the coccyx.

The surgeon begins constructing the eye gap flap by defining a skin paddle centered around the inferior gluteal artery perforation, located between the posterior superior iliac spine and the ischial tuberosity at the piriformis level. Incisions are made through the subcutaneous and fascial layers down to the gluteus maximus muscle, carefully avoiding damage to underlying structures. The perforating vessels are identified and mobilized from the gluteus maximus muscle by dissecting along the fiber travel line. The flap is mobilized laterally to medially in the sub-facial layer, ensuring good rotation for the flap.

The surgeon advances the eye gap flap into the perineal defect, identifying and de-epithelializing the buried portion. Multiple perforating vessels, including an artery and a vein, are visible passing through the gluteus maximus muscle into the flap. The flap is secured superiorly to the superficial part of the sacrum and inferiorly to the pelvic inlet, ensuring well-vascularized tissue at the wound base. A suction drain is placed underneath the flap, extending to the base of the perineal defect, and the wound is closed in layers using a VY fashion.

There are a number of important post-operative considerations in patients who have received an eye gap flap. After surgery, a supine position is avoided for the first twenty four hours and sitting is avoided for the first four days. The flaps are reviewed daily by the plastics and colorectal teams. The patients are allowed to mobilise on the first post-operative day as part of an enhanced recovery programme. Patients are placed in a compression garment continuously for two weeks and then during the day for the following two weeks. The eye gap flap represents a straightforward and reliable option for reconstructing perineal tissue defects. It offers consistent tissue coverage with reduced donor site morbidity, which is important for patients who have often undergone multiple previous operations. The eye gap flap should be an important consideration for coloprotologists who manage patients suffering from significant perineal tissue loss.
